content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 7, 1993

Judith Martinl
Portola City Clerk
P.O. Box 1225
Portola, CA 96122

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-378

Dear Ms. Martinl:

You have requested advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTIONS

1. Do individuals incur reporting obligations if they make
expenditures in connection with a petition that is required to be
submitted to a Local Agency Formation Commission ("LAFCO") as part
of the process to place a measure on the ballot to disincorporate
the city of Portola?

2. If the issue is placed on the ballot, where would
committee campaign statements be filed?

3. What are your duties as the city clerk of Portola?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not require campaign statements to be filed
until the board of supervisors grants approval to submit the
disincorporation issue to the voters. It is at that time that the
Proposal will become a "measure." If the issue becomes a measure,
a group of citizens will qualify as a committee and must file
campaign statements if they raise or spend $1,000 or more to
influence citizens to vote for the ballot measure.

1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

A-93-378
Page 2


2.     Committee statements to support or oppose the ballot
measure must be filed with the city clerk of Portola.

3.     Section 81010 and Regulation 18110 list the duties
required of a campaign filing officer.

FACTS

     You are the city clerk of Portola and are concerned about
campaign filing obligations with respect to a petition effort to
disincorporate the city of Portola.  In telephone conversations
with me on October 20, 1993 and October 27, 1993, you confirmed
that citizens are currently gathering petition signatures for
submission to LAFCO.  These citizens have made expenditures for
newspaper advertisements and professionally printed posters that
urge citizens to sign the LAFCO petitions.  In order for the issue
to be placed on the ballot, LAFCO must grant approval and submit
the issue to the Plumas County Board of Supervisors.  This Board
has sole authority to place the issue on the ballot.  Only
registered voters residing in Portola may vote.

ANALYSIS

     The Act provides that campaign disclosure obligations will be
incurred by persons who make payments for political purposes.
(Section 82013 and 82025.)  A payment is made for political
purposes if it is intended to influence the actions of the voters
for or against the qualification or passage of any measure.
(Regulation 18225.)

     The Commission has held that a proponent's obligation to
report expenditures in support of a ballot proposal begins when
the proposal becomes a "measure."  (In re Fontana (1976) 2 FPPC
Ops. 25.)  With regard to incorporation (or disincorporation)
matters, that does not occur until the Board of Supervisors places
the proposal on the ballot.  (Id. at 28.)  However, the commission
also clearly stated that although the proponent need not file
reports until that time, previous contributions and expenditures
may need to be recaptured on the first campaign report.

     (The) initial campaign statement must include
contributions received and expenditures made in
anticipation of the measure being placed on the ballot,
even if such contributions and expenditures were made
before the Board of Supervisors actually placed the
proposal on the ballot.
                                        (Id. at 28 n.5.)

     Monies received and spent by a proponent in gathering
petitions to submit to LAFCO are not reportable.  However,
contributions and expenditures that predate the filing of the
first campaign statement are reportable if they were intended to
influence voters to vote for disincorporation.

A-93-378
Page 3


     If the issue is placed on the ballot and a citizen group
raises or spends $1,000 or more to influence voters to support the
ballot measure, the group will qualify as a committee and must
file campaign statements. (Sections 82103(a) and 84200-84211.)
Because only Portola residents will vote on the ballot measure,
required campaign statements must be filed with your office.
(Section 84215(e).)

     Section 81009 lists the duties of a campaign filing officer
as follows:

          (a) Supply the necessary forms and manuals prescribed
     by the Commission;
          (b) Determine whether required documents have been
     filed and, if so, whether they conform on their face with the
     requirements of this title;
          (c) Notify promptly all persons and known committees
     who have failed to file a report or statement in the form and
     at the time required by this title;
          (d) Report apparent violations of this title to the
     appropriate agencies; and
          (e) Compile and maintain a current list of all reports
     and statements filed with this office.

     Regulation 18110 and additional informational material is
included in the enclosed campaign disclosure handbook for city and
county filing officers. Also, I have enclosed the Jorgenson
Advice Letter, No. A-92-406, which clarifies the duties of a
campaign filing officer.

     If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        by: Lynda Cassady
                                            Assistant Division Chief
                                            Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures
